does: This paper introduces SciSafeEval, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating safety alignment of large language models across scientific tasks. The benchmark spans multiple scientific languages including natural text, molecular structures, protein sequences, and genomic data, covering domains like biology, chemistry, medicine, and physics. The authors evaluate LLMs under various prompting conditions—zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought reasoning—and incorporate a jailbreak enhancement feature designed to test the robustness of safety guardrails against adversarial attacks. The benchmark provides systematic measurements of how well LLMs resist attempts to elicit harmful outputs while maintaining scientific reasoning capabilities.
connects: This work builds on the growing recognition that while LLMs demonstrate impressive capabilities in scientific applications, their safety properties in specialized scientific contexts remain inadequately understood. It extends beyond existing safety benchmarks like HELM and TrustLLM, which primarily focus on general textual safety concerns and lack coverage of domain-specific scientific modalities. The paper connects to the intersection of AI safety alignment research and scientific ML, where concerns about misuse of models in sensitive domains like synthetic biology, drug discovery, and genomics have become increasingly prominent. It also draws on emerging jailbreaking literature that identifies vulnerabilities in safety mechanisms through adversarial prompting.
enables: This benchmark enables more rigorous evaluation and improvement of safety mechanisms tailored to scientific LLM applications. It supports researchers in identifying domain-specific vulnerabilities in safety guardrails that may differ from general-domain safety issues, potentially revealing failure modes unique to scientific reasoning chains or multimodal scientific representations. The work paves the way for developing enhanced safety training methods that account for scientific task specificity, including techniques that maintain both scientific capability and safety alignment. It also establishes a foundation for future work on safety metrics and defenses in emerging areas like AI-assisted synthetic biology, clinical decision support, and computational drug design where LLM deployment carries significant real-world consequences.
